Municipal Clerk   Council President  Adopted:   July 16,   2013  148-13  THIS   AGREEMENT,   made this   day of   July   20 13   by and  between:  TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE, a municipal corporation of the  State of New Jersey, having its principal offices at 66   Main  Street,   West Orange, New Jersey,   hereinafter referred to as  "Township";   and  POLICE   ATHLETIC LEAGUE, a nonprofit   corporation of   the  State of New Jersey having its principal   offices at Prospect  Place,   West   Orange, New Jersey, hereinafter referred to   as  " PAL ".  W I T N   E S S   E T H:  WHEREAS,   the Township desires to provide certain  recreational services,   social services, activities, and  facilities to the residents of the Township of West Orange;  and  WHEREAS,   it is the purpose   of this   agreement to recite  the terms and conditions under which the   foregoing shall be  provided; and  NOW THEREFORE , in consideration of the   foregoing and of  the mutual covenants hereinafter   stated,   the parties do  hereby   agree as   follows:

1.   F or the period   of January 1, 2013   to  December 31, 2013   PAL   shall provide and   make available to  West   Orange   residents,   services and facilities for  participation by such residents of West Orange.  2.   The   foregoing facilities   and services shall be  supervised and operated by personnel provided by   PAL ,  wh ose   personnel   shall be solely the employees of   the  volunteers of PAL   who is responsible to   pay them , where  appropriate, at   the sole discretion of   PAL .  3.   PAL   shall provide, at   PAL   expense,   Workers  Compensation Insurance, covering   its employees  performing hereunder, and   public   liability insurance with  limits   of Five Hundred   Thousand ($500,000.00) Dollars to One  Million   ($1,000,000.00) Dollars for the areas and locations  where the recreational   facilities are conducted. Said  insurance shall   cover the   interest of the Township and shall  be   with   carriers satisfactory to   the Township and authorized  to   do business in New Jersey. Certificates evidencing the  foregoing shall   be   furnished to the Township upon execution  of   this   Agreement.  4.   PAL   agrees   to indemnify the   Township and save  it   harmless, including   Township's employees, agents and  servants, from and   against any and all losses, claims,  liabilities   and   expenses which may arise   or be claimed  against   the   Township, its   agents, servants or employees   in  connection with   any phase   of this Agreement.

5.   Neither   this Agreement nor the monies to grow  hereunder may be assigned.  6.   PAL   acknowledges that it has read N.J.S.A. 10:2-1,  et   seq., of   the   Revised Statutes   of the State of New Jersey  (Discrimination   in   Employment on   Public Works, Contract  Provisions), the terms   of   which are incorporated herein   as  though   expressly set forth at length.   PAL   agrees to comply  with   and be   bound by the provisions thereof.  7.   It is understood that this Agreement shall be deemed  effective as of   January 1,2013 .  8.   Township shall   pay to   PAL   for the services to be  provided hereunder   for   the full one-year   term, the sum of  Twelve   Thousand   ($ 12 , 0 00.00) Dollars.  9.   The manner   of payment shall   be as determined jointly  by   the   President of PAL and Director of the Department of  Recreation of Township.  10.   The   parties hereto agree   that the provisions of   the  “ New   Jersey Prevailing Wage Act ”   shall   apply hereto the  extent   held   applicable   to   a volunteer organization.  Accordingly, PAL agrees to comply with the provisions of  N.J.S.A. 34:11-56.25 through 34:11-56.33, the provisions of  which are hereby in corporated by reference as though stated  at   length herein.   PAL   hereby declares that it is aware   of  the prevailing wage rates   on file in the   Township Hall   of  Township pursuant to said   Act and agrees   to comply therewith  to   the   extent held   applicable to   volunteer organizations.

PAL further   agrees   as follows:  A.   All   covered employees of   PAL performing   services  hereunder shall   be   paid not less   than the prevailing wage  rate   as   specified.  B . PAL shall maintain accurate   records, showing  name, kind of work   performed and   actual hourly rate of wages  paid   to   each employee employed hereunder. Such records shall  be   maintained for three (3) years from date of payment and  shall be available   for   inspection by the   Township's Business  Administrator or his designee on   request.  C.   PAL shall post the   prevailing wage rate for  each   employee performing worker hereunder, as determined by  the Commissioner of Labor   and Industry of the State of New  Jersey,   including the effective date of any changes thereof,  in   a   prominent and   easily   accessible place and the principal  Office   of PAL.  D.   If it is found   that any employee   of PAL   is  paid   less than the   required wage   rates Township   may, on  written   notice to PAL,   terminate   PAL's rights  to   proceed hereunder, or as to such portion of services  rendered hereunder   as to which there has   been a   failure   to  pay the   required wages. Township   may then arrange to  prosecute the rendering of the services hereunder by other  means,   in which   even PAL shall be liable   to Township  for all   costs and damages   incurred by Township.  E.   It is specifically understood that the provisions of

this   paragraph shall not apply to such persons engaged by  PAL render services hereunder who are performing such  services as   volunteers   without remuneration.  F. PAL   agrees   to be bound by the provisions of   Schedule  A attached hereto and made a part hereof. (IV)   The Contractor or subcontractor   agrees   to revise  any  of   its   testing procedure,   if necessary, to assure that all  personnel testing conforms with the principles of job-  related  testing, as   established by the statutes and court decisions  of   the   State of   New Jersey and as established by applicable  federal   law   and   applicable federal court   decisions.  (V)   The   contractor   or   subcontractor agrees to review all  procedures relating to   transfer,   upgrading, downgrading   and  layoff   to ensure that all   such actions are taken without  regard   to age, creed, color, national origin, ancestry,  marital   status or sex,   and conform with the applicable  employment goals, consistent with the statutes and court  decisions of the State   of   New Jersey, and applicable federal  Law and   applicable   federal court   decisions.

150-13  July 16, 2013  R E S O L U T I O N  WHEREAS   ,   the Township of West Orange needs to replace a deteriorated storm drain system  that runs through properties at 24 Curtis Avenue, 30 Curtis Avenue and 2 Roosevelt Avenue; and  WHEREAS,   this drainage system serves portions of Roosevelt Avenue, Garfield Avenue and  Cleveland Terrace; and  WHERAS,   no record of an easement exists for this storm drain system; and  WHEREAS,   the Township Engineer determined new easements will allow for a better alignment  of the replacement storm sewer system through 30 Curtis Avenue, Block 175.19, Lot 28 and 2 Curtis  Avenue, Block 175.19, Lot 3; and  WHEREAS,   the new alignment and easements will avoid the costs associated with the removal  and replacement of existing shade trees on private property; and  WHEREAS,   the consideration offered for these easements will at least be partially offset by the  costs avoided by not removing and replacing trees; and  WHEREAS,   the Township Engineer prepared two Easement Agreements and Deeds of Easement  between Bessie Kra, 30 Curtis Avenue, West Orange, N.J. Block 175.19, Lot 28 and the Township and  Mindy Schwartzman, 2 Curtis Avenue, West Orange, N.J. Block 175.19, Lot 3 and the Township; and  WHEREAS,   the considerations and other fees associated with these Easement Agreements are  $4,545.00 and $1,360.00 respectively; and  WHEREAs,   these Easement Agreements and Deeds of Easement are acceptable to Mrs. Bessie  Kra, Mrs. Mindy Schwartzman and the Township Engineer; and  WHEREAS,   the Township Engineer recommends the Township Council authorize the execution  of these Agreements; and  WHEREAS,   the Township Council concurs in the foregoing.  NOW, THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED   by the Township Council of the Township of West  Orange that the Easement Agreements and Deed of Easements between Bessie Kra, 30 Curtis Avenue,  West Orange, N.J., Block 175.19, Lot 28 and the Township for a consideration of $4,545.00 and Mindy  Schwartzman, 2 Roosevelt Avenue, West Orange, N.J. Block 175.19, Lot 3 for a total consideration of  $1,360.00 are hereby approved and Mayor Robert Parisi is authorized to sign the Agreements and the  Municipal Clerk, Karen Carnevale, shall attest his signature.

________________________________  _____________________________________  KAREN J. 151-13  July 16, 2013  R E S O L U T I O N  WHEREAS,   the   Township   of   West   Orange   requested   proposals   from   six   (6)   Engineering   and  Land Surveying firms to provide design services for the Improvement of Moore Terrace; and  WHEREAS,   five (5) firms responded to the Request for Proposals; and  WHEREAS,   the   services   of   a   professional   engineer   and   land   surveyor   are   required   to   provide   the  necessary surveys and preparation of improvement plans and specifications; and  WHEREAS,   Suburban   Consulting   Engineers,   Inc.   100   Valley   Road,   Suite   202,   Mount   Arlington,  New   Jersey   submitted   a   proposal   dated   June   28,   2013   to   provide   Engineering   and   Land   Surveying  Services   for   a   fee   not   to   exceed   $20,290.00   which   specifically   excluded   Traffic   Control   and   Police   Traffic  Directors; and  WHEREAS,   the proposal is acceptable to the Municipal Engineer; and  WHEREAS,   the   Municipal   Engineer   recommends   an   allowance   of   $2,000.00   be   added   to   the  proposal fee for Police Traffic Directors; and  WHEREAS,   this   project   may   be   funded   by   a   Municipal   Aid   or   Discretionary   Aid   Grant   from   the  New Jersey Transportation Trust Fund; and  WHEREAS,   the Township Council concurs in the foregoing.  NOW   THEREFORE   BE   IT   RESOLVED,   by   the   Township   Council   of   the   Township   of   West  Orange   that   the   Township   be   and   hereby   authorized   to   enter   into   a   Professional   Services   Contract   with  Suburban   Consulting   Engineers,   Inc.,   100   Valley   Road,   Suite   202,   Mount   Arlington,   New   Jersey   07856   to  provide   Engineering   and   Land   Surveying   services   for   the   project   the   Improvement   of   Moore   Terrace   for   a  fee   not   to   exceed   $22,290.00   which   includes   a   $2,000.00   allowance   for   Police   Traffic   Directors   all   in  accordance with their proposal dated June 28, 2013.

ORDINANCE   2378-13  AN   ORDINANCE   AMEND ING   AND   SUPPLEMENTING   CHAPTER   3,   SECTION   5   OF   THE  GENERAL ORDINANCES OF THE TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE, ENTITLED   “ A GENDA ”  BE   IT   ORDAINED   BY   THE   TOWNSHIP   COUNCIL   OF   THE   TOWNSHIP   OF   WEST  ORANGE ,   in   the   County   of   Essex   and   Stat e   of   New   Jersey,   that   Chapter   3,   Section   5   of   the  Revised   General   Ordinances   of   the   Township   of   West   Orange   is   hereby   amended   and  supplemented as follows:  I.   PURPOSE  The purpose of this Ordinance is to   make clearer the Agenda and posting process.  II.   CHAPTER   3,   SECTION   5   SHALL   BE   AND   HEREBY   IS   AMENDED   AND  SUPERSEDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS:  An   agenda   for   each   regular   meeting   of   the   Township   Council   shall   be   prepared   by   the  Clerk   under   the   supervision   of   the   President   of   the   Council.   All   Ordinances,   Resolutions   and   any  supporting   written   material   for   consideration   at   the   next   Council   Meeting   shall   be   delivered   to  and   received   at   the   Clerk's   Office   by   3:00   p.m.,   on   the   Wednesday   next   preceding   the   meeting,  earlier   if   possible   and,   if   later,   shall   require   permission   of   the   Council   President   before  placement   on   the   agenda.   The   agenda   headings   shall   be   written   in   plain   English   and   each  Resolution   and   Ordinance,   unless   clearly   self-explanatory,   shall   include   a   brief   description   of   its  purpose   also   written   in   plain   English   by   the   department   submitting   it.   The   agenda   shall   be  completed   not   later   than   Noon   on   the   Thursday   preceding   each   regular   meeting   of   the   Council,  except   for   emergency   matters   which   may   be   added   to   the   agenda   at   any   time   before   the  meeting   with   permission   of   the   President   of   the   Council.   The   Clerk   shall   expeditiously   deliver   a  copy   of   the   Agenda,   all   Ordinances   and   Resolutions   and   all   submitted   supporting   materials,   to  each   Councilmember,   to   the   Mayor,   Business   Administrator,   and   to   the   Law   Department.   The  Clerk   shall   expeditiously   cause   the   agenda,   including   the   Ordinances,   Resolutions   and   all  submitted   supporting   materials,   to   be   posted   on   the   Township's   website   no   later   than   the  following   Friday   morning   in   order   that   such   materials   are   readily   available   to   the   general   public.  It   shall   be   the   responsibility   of   the   Administration   to   determine   if   any   submitted   supporting  materials   contain   information   which   is   privileged,   confidential,   or   for   some   lawful   reason   should  not   be   published   at   that   time.   Such   supporting   materials   shall   not   be   posted   on   the   Township’s  website   or   otherwise   disclosed   to   the   public   until   the   reasons   for   non-disclosure   no   longer   exist.  In   the   event   of   an   intervening   holiday,   the   deadlines   set   forth   here   shall   be   adjusted   to   provide  the   same   amount   of   time   for   preparation   and   notice   of   the   agenda.   In   the   event   of   additions,  deletions,   or   changes   of   any   kind   to   the   agenda,   or   any   item   on   it,   such   changes   shall   be  expeditiously   posted   to   the   Township's   website   with   a   notation   indicating   the   change   by   4:30  p.m.,   on   the   Monday   preceding   the   Council   meeting.   The   agenda   and   any   changes   to   it   shall  also   be   physically   posted   on   the   Clerk's   Bulletin   Board   in   the   public   hallway   at   Town   Hall.   A  conforming   agenda   shall   be   published   to   the   Township's   website   on   the   day   after   each   Council  meeting   to   indicate   those   items   which   were   not   acted   upon   due   to   removal   or   postponement   by  the Council at its meeting.   (1972 Code § 3-5; Ord. No. 2349-12)

2  III.   REPEAL OF CONFLICTING ORDINANCES  Any   Ordinances   of   the   Township,   which   are   in   conflict   with   this   Ordinance,   are   hereby  repealed to the extent of such conflict.  IV.   SEVERABILITY  If   any   part   of   this   Ordinance   shall   be   deemed   invalid,   such   parts   shall   be   severed   and  the invalidity thereof shall not affect the remaining parts of this Ordinance.  V.   EFFECTIVE DATE  This   Ordinance   shall   take   effect   upon   final   passage   and   publication   in   accordance   with  the law.  __________________________________  Honorable   Susan McCartney  Council President  ___________________________________  Honorable Robert D. Parisi  Mayor  Introduced :   July 16, 2013  Adopted: August 20, 2013  Approved as to form on the basis of the facts provided:  _________________________________________  Karen J. Carnevale, Municipal Clerk

3  2378-13  LEGISLATIVE HISTORY  This   proposed   Ordinance   is   intended   to   further   clarify   the   process   and   procedure  regarding   the   delivery   and   availability   of   materials   to   be   considered   by   the   Township   Council   in  connection   with   each   Council   meeting.   There   has   been   Council   time   expended   discussing   what  materials   should   be   publicly   available,   and   in   order   to   more   expeditiously   address   matters   of  business   before   the   Council,   it   is   necessary   for   the   to   ensure   a   uniform   procedure   for   the   timely  submission and   publicly-available   posting of   agenda   materials.  This   proposed   Ordinance   would   also   require   the   Township’s   administration   to   timely  verify   which   materials   being   presented   to   the   Council   for   consideration   contain   privileged   or  confidential   information   which   should   not   be   publicly   disclosed .   Ultimately ,   this   Ordinance   is  necessary   and   proper,   intended   to   establish   clear   and   uniform   procedures   for   submission   and  consideration   of   agenda   materials   for   the   start   of   the   2013   Council   term ,   and   each   year  thereafter,   unless and   until the Ordinance is further amended.  743017v1

2380-13  ORDINANCE   OF   THE   TOWNSHIP   OF   WEST   ORANGE ,   IN   THE  COUNTY   OF   ESSEX ,   STATE   OF   NEW   JERSEY   ADOPTING   A N  AMENDMENT TO THE   HARVARD PRESS   REDEVELOPMENT   PLAN  WHEREAS,   the   Local   Redevelopment   and   Housing   Law,   N.J.S.A.   40A:12A-1,   et   seq .,  (the   “Act”)   authorizes   municipalities   to   determine   whether   certain   parcels   of   land   in   the  municipality constitute areas in need of redevelopment; and  WHEREAS,   after   referral   to   and   investigation   by   the   Planning   Board   of   the   Township   in  accordance   with   the   Act,   on   March   18 ,   2008,   the   West   Orange   Township   Council,   acting   by  resolution,   designated   the   following   area   as   an   area   in   need   of   redevelopment   in   accordance   with  the   Act:   Block   9,   Lot   1,   commonly   known   as   9   Mitchell   Street;   Block   9,   Lot   7,   commonly  known   as   15   Mitchell   Street;   Block   9,   Lot   44,   commonly   known   as   22-24   Central   Avenue;   Block  9,   Lot   50,   commonly   known   as   24-26   Central   Avenue;   and   Block   9,   Lot   56,   located   on   Central  Avenue   (collectively,   and   together   with   any   roads,   right-of-ways   or   other   public   lands  encompassed therein or appurtenant thereto, the   “ Redevelopment Area ” ); and  WHEREAS,   on   April   29,   2008,   after   review   of   a   proposed   redevelopment   plan   for   the  Redevelopment   Area   by   the   Planning   Board   of   the   Township   in   accordance   with   the   Act,   and  after   reviewing   the   recommendations   of   the   Planning   Board   with   respect   to   the   same,   the  Township   Council   finally   adopted   Ordinance   #   2168-08   implementing   the   proposed  redevelopment   plan   together   with   the   Planning   Board’s   recommendations,   as   reflected   in   the  Ordinance (the   “ Redevelopment Plan ” ); and  WHEREAS,   acting   by   Resolution   adopted   June   25,   2013,   the   Township   referred   certain  proposed   amendments   to   the   Redevelopment   Plan   as   set   forth   at   Exhibit   A   attached   hereto   (the  “Proposal”)   to   the   Planning   Board   for   its   review   and   comment   in   accordance   with   N.J.S.A.  40A:12A-7 (e) ; and  WHEREAS ,   on   July   9,   2013 ,   the   Planning   Board   conducted   a   public   meeting   regarding  the   Proposal,   recommended   the   adoption   of   the   Proposal   and   resolved   to   transmit   its  recommendation   to   the   Mayor   and   Council   (such   resolution   of   the   Planning   Board   attached  hereto as   Exhibit B ) ; and  WHEREAS ,   the   Township   desire s   to adopt the   Proposal,  NOW,   THEREFORE,   BE   IT   ORDAINED   BY   THE   TOWNSHIP   COUNCIL   OF  THE TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE   AS FOLLOWS :  I.   GENERAL  The aforementioned recitals are incorporated herein as though fully set forth at length.

II.   ADOPTION OF PLAN   AMENDMENT  The   Propos al ,   as   filed   in   the   Office   of   the   Township   Clerk   and   attached   hereto   as   Exhibit  A , is hereby approved .  III.   AMENDMENT OF ZONING ORDINANCE  The   zoning   ordinance   and   map   of   the   Township   is   hereby   amended   to   incorporate   the  provisions of the Propos al .  IV.   SEVERABILITY  If   any   part   of   this   Ordinance   shall   be   deemed   invalid,   such   parts   shall   be   severed   and   the  invalidity thereby shall not affect the remaining parts of this Ordinance.  V.   AVAILABILITY OF THE ORDINANCE  A   copy   of   this   Ordinance   shall   be   available   for   public   inspection   at   the   offices   of   the  Township Clerk.  VI.   EFFECTIVE DATE  This Ordinance shall take effect as provided in law.  ____________________________________  Honorable   Susan McCartney  Council President  ____________________________________  Honorable Robert Parisi  Mayor  Introduced:   July 16, 2013  Adopted:  Approved as to form on the basis of the facts provided:  ____________________________________  Karen J. 2381-13  AN ORDINANCE OF THE TOWNSHIP OF   WEST ORANGE   , COUNTY OF   ESSEX ,  STATE OF NEW JERSEY,  AUTHORIZING THE ESTABLISHMENT OF A GOVERNMENT ENERGY AGGREGATION  PROGRAM  WHEREAS,   the Government Energy   Aggregation Act,   N.J.S.A. 48:3-93.1   et seq.   governs  the establishment of a government energy aggregation program, which is a government-  operated purchasing cooperative through which multiple energy   consumers purchase energy  together   under the auspices of a government aggregator; and  WHEREAS,   the New Jersey Board of Public Utilities   ( “ BPU ” )   has promulgated rules  (N.J.A.C. 14:4-6) for the implementation of government energy aggregation programs; and  WHEREAS   , pursuant to the Government Energy Aggregation Act,   N.J.S.A.   48:3-93.1   et  seq. ,   the Township seeks to establish a Government Energy Aggregation Program ( “ Program ” )  for the provision of electricity within the Township , for the purpose of obtaining power supply  cost savings for residential participants in the Township ; and  WHEREAS,   the West Orange   Township Council intends to   approve   a Government  Energy Aggregation Program in   the   Township and   authorize the Mayor and/or Township  personnel,   consistent with   any   a pplicable rules, to solicit proposals   and enter into a contract for  the provision of electric generation services on behalf of residential customers within the  boundaries of the Township, to the extent that such a contract will produce power supply cost  savings to residents; and  WHEREAS,   the Township   may   from time to time   solicit proposals   from electric power  suppliers for electric generation services through the Program in which the Township   and/or  authorized personnel   will   be lead agents in administering   the   West OrangeCommunity Energy  Aggregation ( “ WO CEA ” ); and  WHEREAS,   the Township   may   from time to time issue one or more Request for  Proposals for electric generation services and energy aggregation services on behalf of the  WO CEA pursuant to the Local Public Contract Law , the Government Energy Aggregation Act  and   the   Electric Discount and Energy Competition Act ; and  WHEREAS,   the Township will award contracts for said electric generation service and  energy aggregation services to electric power suppliers that are deemed qualified and that  submit price bids lower than the prevailing price for utility-provided basic generation service;  and  WHEREAS   , pursuant to   applicable BPU rules , the Township needs to enter into an  Electric Distribution Company Aggregation Agreement with   Public Service Electric and Gas  Company ; and  WHEREAS   ,   N.J.S.A.   48:3-93.1   et seq.   requires the Program to be established by  ordinance ;  NOW,   THEREFORE,   BE   IT   ORDAINED   BY   THE   TOWNSHIP   COUNCIL   OF   THE  TOWNSHIP   OF   WEST   ORANGE   ,   in   the   County   of   Essex   and   State   of   New   Jersey,   that   there

should   be   created   a   Chapter   2 ,   Section   93 ,   of   the   Revised   General   Ordinances   of   the   Township  of West Orange, which shall provide as follows:  TOWNSHIP CODE   §   2-93.1  2-93. 1 : The Township hereby establishes a Government Energy Aggregation Program, in  accordance with the provisions of the Government Energy Aggregation Act,   N.J.S.A.   48:3-93.1  et seq .  2-93.2:   The Mayor shall be and hereby   is authorized to execute and the Clerk to attest to  the execution of an Electric Distribution Aggregation Agreement, in a form acceptable to the  Town s hip, with   Public Service Electric   and   Gas   Company   ( “ PSE&G ” ) .  2-93.3:   The   Township Council   may oversee   the WO CEA . The Mayor and appropriate  Township employees   or other authorized personnel , consistent with applicable rules,   shall   be  lead agents and   solicit proposals for electric generation service and energy aggregation  services on behalf of Township residents . The Mayor may execute and   enter into a contract for  such services provided that the lowest qualified bid price is below the prevailing price for utility-  provided basic generation service , consistent with any applicable law .  2-93.4:   The Mayor may also   execute a master perf ormance agreement that obligates   the  participants in the   WO CEA to purchase electricity at terms and conditions stated therein with a  third party supplier who has been awarded the contract by the   Township   on behalf of  participating members of the   WO CEA, and provided that such contract shall be at prices  reasonably forecast and estimated by the   Township to provide savings to participants relative to  the price charged for basic generation service by   PSE&G .  2-93.5:   All ordinances or parts of ordinances inconsistent herewith are hereby repealed.  2-93.6:   If any section, subsection, sentence, clause, phrase or portion of this ordinance is  for any reason held to be invalid or unconstitutional   by a court of compete nt jurisdiction , such  portion shall be deemed a separate, distinct and independent provision, and such holding shall  not affect the validity of the remaining portions hereof.  ___________________________________  Honorable Robert D. Parisi,   Mayor  Honorable Susan McCartney  Council President  Karen J. Carnevale, Municipal Clerk  Introduced: July 16, 2013  Adopted:

126-13  June   25 , 2013  RESOLUTION  WHEREAS,   the   Township   of   West   Orange   is   desirous   to   implement   a   Government   Energy  Aggregation   (GEA)   program   for   Township   residents.   The   GEA   program   will   allow   West   Orange  residents   to   realize   a   savings   on   their   gas   and   electric   bills   while   remaining   a   PSE&G   consumer;  and  WHEREAS,   the   services   of   a   professional   consulting   firm   is   essential   to   aid   the   Township   with  all the steps necessary to implement a Township wide program of this nature; and  WHEREAS,   the   Township   did   advertise   for   such   services   and   on   the   submission   date   of   April  10,   2013   did   receive   one   proposal   from   Gabel   Associates,   Inc.,   417   Denison   Street,   Highland  Park, New Jersey 08904; and  WHEREAS,   Gabel   Associates   is   a   qualified   Government   Energy   Aggregation   Consultant   who  has   demonstrated   a   positive   track   record   related   to   electric   and   natural   gas   procurement   on   behalf  of the local units in the State of New Jersey, and  WHEREAS,   there   will   be   no   cost   to   the   Township   for   these   services   and   an   agreement  attachment A is a part of this resolution.  NOW,   THEREFORE   BE   IT   RESOLVED   by   the   Township   Council   of   the   Township   of   West  Orange   that   a   professional   services   contract   is   awarded   to   the   firm   Gabel   Associates,   Inc.   to  provide   Government   Energy   Aggregation   services   in   accordance   with   their   proposal   and  agreement attached hereto.  BE   IT   FURTHER   RESOLVED   that   a   copy   of   this   resolution   be   published   in   the   West   Orange  Chronicle as required by law within ten (10) days of its passage.  Karen J. Carnevale   Victor Cirilo  Municipal Clerk   Council President  Adopted: June   25 , 2013

(Gabel   Associates  or   Consultant)   having   its   principal   place   of   business   at   417   Denison   Street,   Highland  Park, New Jersey 08904, hereby recites as follows:  WITNESSETH:  WHEREAS,   West   Orange   designated   Gabel   Associates   as   a   provider   of   energy  agent   services   to   be   utilized   by   West   Orange   in   connection   with   the   development   and  administration   of   energy   procurement   processes   for   a   Government   Energy   Aggregation  (GEA)   program   to   be   conducted   on   an   on-line   basis   pursuant   to   P.L.   2001,   c.   30;   N.J.S.A.  48:3-50-51; and 48:3-92 et. al.; and  WHEREAS,   Gabel   Associates   is   willing   and   able   to   provide   the   required   services  (with such services outlines fully below); now, therefore,  IN   CONSIDERATION   of   the   mutual   promises   contained   herein,   West   Orange   and  the Consultant agree as follows:  1.   TERM  The   term   of   this   Agreement   shall   be   for   the   period   commencing   upon   Effective  Date   of   this   Agreement   until   execution   of   a   contract   with   a   suitable   vendor   is   signed  pursuant   to   the   terms   and   scope   of   services   of   this   Agreement,   or   for   a   twelve   (12)   month  term ,   whichever   is   shorter.   The   12-month   term   may   be   renewed   or   extended   only   by  mutual agreement   or resolution ,   as may be   permitted by law.

Agreement by and Between Township of West Orange, NJ and Gabel Associates  Government Energy Aggregation Consulting Services -  Page   2  2.   CONSULTING SERVICES  The   Consulting   Services   (also   referred   to   as   the   "Services")   to   be   performed  by   Gabel   Associates   and   its   subcontractor,   Cooling   America   Thru   Local   Leadership  (CALL),   that   are   the   subject   of   this   Agreement   are   as   follows.   Gabel   shall   be   responsible  for   all   actions   of   CALL.   All   Services   under   this   Agreement   shall   be   undertaken   pursuant  to   direction   from   West   Orange   or   its   project   manager   employees ,   and   subject   to   West  Orange ’ s review and approval .  These   services   relate   to   electricity   and   may   include   other   energy   services  including   but   not   limited   to   natural   gas   supply   service,   may   be   conducted   separately   (in  the event that a GEA does not move forward for a particular service) or as one process.  A) Government Energy Aggregation Services (GEA):  Gabel   and   CALL   shall   assist   West   Orange   in   organizing   a   GEA   program.   A  GEA   program   is   as   defined   in   N.J.S.A.   48:3-92   et   al.   Such   a   program   is   not   a   standard  energy   procurement   process   but   requires   the   involvement   of   West   Orange   residents,   local  governments,   the   Board   of   Public   Utilities   (BPU)   regulators   and   Third   Party   Suppliers  (TPS).   The   duties   of   Gabel   and/or   CALL   shall   include,   but   are   in   no   way   limited   to,   the  following:  1.   PUBLIC OUTREACH     Work with West Orange to set up and advertise public discussion forms  about GEA     Develop mailing describing the program and inviting residents and  businesses to attend town hall meetings for more information     Hold   town-hall   meetings   to   present   and   discuss   the   concept   of   GEA   with  residents   and   businesses,   the   scheduling   of   said   meetings   to   be  coordinated with Township officials     Work with West Orange to target businesses that may want to "opt in"

Agreement by and Between Township of West Orange, NJ and Gabel Associates  Government Energy Aggregation Consulting Services -  Page   6  Council   authorizing   the cancellation .  4.   COMPENSATION  For   all   Consulting   Services   rendered   by   Gabel   Associates   pursuant   to   Section   2   of   this  Agreement,   or   by   its   subcontractor(s),   Gabel   and/or   its   subcontractor(s)   Associates   shall  be   paid   by   the   winning   supplier(s).   The   Request   for   Bids   for   Electric   Supply   Service   shall  require   that   Bid   Prices   include   an   energy   agent   fee   of   $.00082 5   per   kilowatt   hour.   The  suppliers   shall   include   this   agent   fee   in   their   bid   prices   and   the   winning   supplier   will  invoice   participants   based   on   the   Bid   Price   each   month   and,   subsequent   to   collection   of  payment   from   participants,   the   winning   supplier   would   remit   payment   of   the   collected  energy   agent   fees   to   Gabel   Associates   on   a   monthly   basis.   Gabel   Associates   will   also  bear   the   cost   of   obtaining   usage   data   for   commercial   customers.   No   management   fees  will   be   collected   by   Gabel   Associates   unless   and   until   a   power   supply   contract   is   awarded  by West Orange.  Certain   expenses   are   required   in   order   to   implement   a   GEA   which   are   not  included   in   the   energy   agent   fee.   These   expenses   are   recoverable   from   the   selected  supplier   pursuant   to   applicable   GEA   Rules.   Two   mailings   are   necessary   for   a   successful  GEA:   the   first   will   serve   to   inform   the   public   that   a   GEA   is   being   implemented   in   West  Orange   and   that   a   public   information   session   is   being   held;   and   the   second   will   be   sent  after   a   winning   supplier   is   chosen   and   will   contain   pricing   and   opt-out   provisions.   Costs  for   the   initial   informational   mailer   will   be   paid   up-front   by   Gabel   Associates   and   recovered  from   the   selected   supplier.   The   second   mailer   will   be   the   responsibility   of   the   selected  supplier.   If   the   RFP   process   does   not   result   in   any   beneficial   proposals   and   as   a   result  the   Township   does   not   award   a   contract   to   a   supplier,   Gabel   Associates   will   absorb   the  costs   associated   with   the   initial   information   mailer.   If   the   RFP   process   does   result   in   a  beneficial   proposal   but   the   Township   decides   that   it   will   nonetheless   not   award   a  contract   to   a   supplier,   Gabel   Associates   will   be   reimbursed   by   West   Orange   for   its   out-of

3  1. Introduction:  The Township of West Orange Mayor and Council requested that a  Redevelopment Plan   be prepared   for   a portion of the Valley Road   area of the Township  of West Orange. The   potential   redevelopment area lies between Mitchell Street and  Central Avenue, east of Valley Road ,   and bordering on the municipal boundary line with  the City of Orange. The area includes five (5) parcels of property, they are:  Block 9, Lot 1;   9 Mitchell Street  Block 9, Lot 7;   15 Mitchell Street  Block 9, Lot 44;   22-24 Central Avenue  Block 9, Lot 50;   24-26 Central Avenue  Block   9,   Lot   56;   Central   Avenue,   having   no   street   frontage  and being located between Lot 1 and Lot 56  On   January   8,   2008   the   Township   Council   of   the   Township   of   West   Orange  adopted   a   Resolution   authorizing   and   directing   the   West   Orange   Planning   Board   to  conduct   a   preliminary   investigation   and   make   a   recommendation   as   to   whether   the  proposed   redevelopment   area   satisfies   the   criteria   of   the   New   Jersey   Local  Redevelopment   and   Housing   Law   (LRHL) ,   N.J.S.A.   40A:12A-1   et   seq.   On   March   5,  2008   th e   area   was   recommended   by   the   Township   of   West   Orange   Planning   Board   to   be  designated   an   Area   in   Need   of   Redevelopment   pursuant   to   N.J.S.A.   40A:12A-6.   As   such ,  these   properties   when   considered   together   as   one   area   satisfy   the   criteria   set   forth   in  N.J.S.A.   40A : 12A-5.   The   Township   Council,   upon   concurring   with   the   recommendation  of   the   West   Orange   Planning   Board,   will   designate   the   area   in   need   of   redevelopment ,  and   upon   such   designation   the   Township   Council   will   refer   this   Redevelopment   Plan   to  the   West   Orange   Planning   Board   for   review   and   recommendation   pursuant   to  N.J.S.A.40A:12A-7e.  The   area   is   located   in   the   southern   portion   of   Downtown   West   Orange,   as  indicated   on   the   Zoning   Map   of   the   Township   of   West   Orange.   T he   former   Harvard   Press  site   is   situated   in   the   area,   which   is   an   abandoned   industrial   area   and   is   subject   to   a  Brownfield ’ s remediation program because of environmental contamin ation .  The   following   map   further   identifies   the   area   to   be   included   in   this  Redevelopment Plan.

4  2. The Redevelopment Plan Statute   and Planning Board Review :  The   New   Jersey   Local   Redevelopment   and   Housing   Law   (LRHL)   N.J.S.A.  40A:12A-1   et   seq.   sets   forth   the   basis   for   determining   if   an   area   qualifies   as   an   area   in  need   of   redevelopment.   In   this   report   we   are   concerned   with   the   statutory   requirements   of  N.J.S.A. 40A:12A-7 .   This section of the LRHL s t ates, in part, that:  “ The   redevelopment   plan   shall   include   an   outline   for   the   planning,   development,  redevelopment, or rehabilitation of the project area sufficient to indicate:  1) Its relationship to definite local objectives as to appropriate land uses, density  of population, and improved traffic and public transportation, public utilities,  recreational and community facilities and other public improvements.  (2) Proposed land uses and building requirements in the project area.

5  (3) Adequate provision for the temporary and permanent relocation, as  necessary, of residents in the project area, including an estimate of the extent to which  decent, safe and sanitary dwelling units affordable to displaced residents will be  available to them in the existing local housing market.  (4) An identification of any property within the redevelopment area which is  proposed to be acquired in accordance with the redevelopment plan.  (5) Any significant relationship of the redevelopment plan to (a) the master plans  of contiguous municipalities, (b) the master plan of the county in which the municipality  is located, and (c) the State Development and Redevelopment Plan adopted pursuant to  the   “ State Planning Act, ”   P.L.1985, c.398 (C.52:18A-196 et al).  b. A redevelopment plan may include the provision of affordable housing in  accordance with the   “ Fair Housing Act, ”   P.L.1985, c.222 (C.52:27D-301 et al.) and the  housing element of the municipal master plan.  c. The redevelopment plan shall describe its relationship to pertinent municipal  development regulations as defined in the   “ Municipal Land Use Law, ”   P.L.1975, c.291  (C.40:55D-1 et seq.). The redevelopment plan shall supersede applicable provisions of  the development regulations of the municipality or constitute an overlay zoning district  within the redevelopment area. When the redevelopment plan supersedes any provision  of the development regulations, the ordinance adopting the redevelopment plan shall  contain an explicit amendment to the zoning district map included in the zoning  ordinance. The zoning district map as amended shall indicate the redevelopment area to  which the redevelopment plan applies. Notwithstanding the provisions of the   “ Municipal  Land Use Law, ”   required for adoption of ordinances by the municipality shall be  required for the hearing on or adoption of the redevelopment plan or subsequent  amendments thereof.  d. All provisions of the redevelopment plan shall be either substantially consistent  with the municipal master plan or designed to effectuate the master plan; but the  municipal governing body may adopt a redevelopment plan which is consistent with or  not designed to effectuate the master plan by affirmative vote of a majority of its full  authorized membership with the reasons for so acting set forth in the redevelopment  plan. ”  This Redevelopment Plan addresses these statutory requirements   and   incorporates  land use   and redevelopment   best practices so   that a high quality plan is achieved   for the  proposed area .  The Draft Valley Road Area (Harvard Press) Redevelopment Plan, dated March  18, 2008, was referred to the West Orange Planning Board by the Township Council. On  April 2, 2008 the   W est Orange Planning Board adopted a resolution enumerating nine  specific recommendations or suggested changes to the Plan. The   recommended   changes  by the Planning Board follow :

6  1.   The   Township   should   consider   reducing   the   maximum   number   of   dwelling   units  per   acre   to   the   maximum   permitted   by   the   Downtown   Redevelopment   Plan  ("Recommendation # 1");  2.   If   a   range   of   unit   types   and   sizes   is   intended   for   the   Area,   the   range   should   be  presented rather than averages ("Recommendation # 2");  3.   Residential   units   in   any   development   for   the   site   should   be   for-sale   only   and  rental units discouraged ("Recommendation # 3");  4.   All   renderings   attached   to   the   Redevelopment   Plan,   in   particular   Exhibit   3   of  Exhibit   A-2   thereto,   should   reflect   the   concepts   contained   therein  ("Recommendation # 4");  5.   The   Township   should   consider   renaming   the   Area   to   remove   the   reference   to  Valley   Road,   as   the   Area   does   not   front   on   Valley   Road   ("Recommendation   #   5");  6.   Residential   Site   Improvement   Standards   for   parking   should   be   applied   in   the   Area  unless otherwise modified by Planning Board ("Recommendation # 6");  7.   A   detailed   economic   impact   analysis   should   be   submitted   to   the   Township   prior   to  execution of a redevelopment agreement for the Area ("Recommendation # 7");  8.   Similarly,   an   analysis   of   the   impact   on   public   safety   from   the   maintenance   of   an  open   waterway   in   the   Area   should   be   submitted   to   the   Township   prior   to   the  execution   of   a   redevelopment   agreement   for   the   Area   ("Recommendation   #   8");  and  9.   Streetscape   improvements   and   infrastructure   guidelines   should   be   required   and  implemented   in   a   manner   similar   to   Downtown   Redevelopment   Plan  ("Recommendation # 9").  This   Final   Plan   includes   the   above   recommendations   ( or   suggested   changes )   with  certain   clarifications,   modifications and minor departures, they are:  1.   As   to   Planning   Board   recommendation   number   4   above :   the   architectural  rendering   in   Exhibit   3   of   the   exhibits   presented   by   Benecke   Economics   to   the  Planning   Board   on   April   2,   2008 ,   was   for   illustrative   purposes   only;  intended   to   show   the   building   and   architectural   detail .   Notwithstanding  the   foregoing   the   building   and   architectural   design   of   any   building   in

7  the Plan area shall be   consistent   with this Plan.  2.   As   to   Planning   Board   recommendation   number   5   above ,   it   is   recommended   that  the Plan be named the Harvard Press Redevelopment Plan.  3.   As to Planning Board recommendation number 6 above, indicating:  Residential   Site   Improvement   Standards   for   parking   should   be   applied   in   the   Area  unless otherwise modified by Planning Board,  requires   clarification ,   so   as   to   provide   specificity   when   reviewing   any   specific  project   in the plan area .  The   Plan   envisions   a   project   with   approximately   100   residential   condominium  units.   The   bedroom   mix   has   not   been   determined,   but   assuming   3  3   units   a re   one  bedroom   units,   63   units   a re   two   bedroom   units   and   the   remainder   as   three  bedroom   units,   strict   adherence   to   the   RSIS   standards   (N.J.A.C.   5:21-4.14)   may  require   as   few   as   115   spaces   and   as   many   as   194   parking   spaces.   T he   RSIS  standard s   takes   into   account   that   where   projects   are   close   to   mass   transit   and   are  located   in   urban   settings,   suc h   as   that   found   in   the   Plan   area ,   it   is   appropriate   to  apply   other wise   applicable   standards   as   permitted   in   N.J.A.C.   5:21-4.14(c),   as  recited   below.   The   Plan   recommends   that   the   130   total   parking   spaces,   100   on   site  and   30   in   close   proximity   to   the   site   be   provided ,   which   is   less   than   “Table   4.4”  parking   requirements   of   RSIS   for   garden   apartments   but   more   than   that   for   high-  rise.   The   RSIS   standards   allow   for   different   local   circumstances   and   Planning  Board shall be guided by N.J.A.C. 5:21-4.14(c), which states:  “Alternative   parking   standards   to   those   shown   in   Table   4.4   shall   be   accepted   if  the   applicant   demonstrates   these   standards   better   reflect   local   conditions.   Factors  affecting   minimum   number   of   parking   spaces   include   household   characteristics,  availability   of   mass   transit,   urban   versus   suburban   location,   and   available   off-site  parking resources. ”  In   the   Plan   area   we   find   the   availability   of   mass   transi t   at   the   Highland   Street   Rail  Station   to   be   an   important   factor,   consistent   with   the   RSIS   standards   noted   in  N.J.A.C.   5:21-4.14(c).   Th e   Plan   area   is   also   urban   as   opposed   to   suburban   and   the  Township   and   any   redeveloper   shall   work   cooperatively   to   provide   additional  parking   resources   in   the   area   pursuant   to   Section   10 - B-2   of   this   Plan.  Accordingly,   the   Plan   does   satisfy   the   RSIS   standards   as   found   in   N.J.A.C.   5:21-

As   to   Planning   Board   recommendations   number   7   and   8:   they   are   already  included   in   the   Plan.   However,   the   waterway   maintenance   issue   should   be  explored   and   an   appropriate   maintenance   program   developed   (see   Section   11   of  this Plan . )  5.   As   to   Planning   Board   recommendation   number   9:   it   was   indicated   at   the   April   2,  2008   West   Orange   Planning   meeting   that   the   building   (architectural   standards)  proposed   in   this   Plan   were   of   high   quality.   The   Main   Street   (Downtown)  Redevelopment   Plan   streetscape   and   infrastructure   i mprovements  guidelines   were   developed   for   a   vibrant   retail   corridor,   having   frontage   on   a   major  artery.   In   this   Plan   we   are   concerned   with   fitting   the   residential   project   into   a   pre-  existing,   sensitive   residential   area.   Notwithstanding   the   foregoing   the   West  Orange   Planning   Board   is   given   the   authority   to   guide   the   project   design   pursuant  Section 10 C of this Plan.  3. The Present   Condition   of the Redevelopment Area:  The p roposed   redevelopment area includes approximately   64 ,000 square feet of  land, approximately 1. 5   acres. Four of the five properties are owned by Wilrick, LLC and  the   fifth   is owned by Mr. Fregans, Block 9, Lot 44. The four properties owned by  Wilrick , LLC   are largely vacant, and in serious disrepair. The   property owned by   Mr.  Fregans , while in operation,   is generally obsolete. Each of the properties   is   under contract  to be sold to   Ha rvard Development   Associates, LLC ,   a   New Jersey based development  company , which   is   also   the   contract   purchaser   of adjacent property located within the  City of Orange , South Jefferson Street . The former Harvard Printing Company structure  located on Block 9, Lots 1 and 56 , is   ostensibly   vacant and in a deleterious condition .   It is  a four   and one half   story   structure   which   continues to the east into the City of Orange.  The boundary line between the Township of West Orange and the City of Orange   runs  through the building.   The City of Orange portion of the Harvard Press structure is located  in the City of Orange, Central Valley Redevelopment Area.

9  The Harvard Press property is a designated Brownfield ’ s site (Brownfield ’ s  Development Area-BDA) and operates under an environmental remediation plan. The  environmental sensitivity of the property is amplified by the fact that the east branch of  the Rahway River runs through the Harvard Press site.  4-Research and Document Review:  As we research ed   this redevelopment area ,   in   prepar ation   of this Redevelopment  Plan, we   made   several site visits and took photographs of the properties located in the  area.   We have conducted an extensive review of pertinent documents. This includes the  West Orange   Master Plan, and Master Plan Re-Examination Report, dated January 7,  2004, the Zoning Map of the Township of West Orange, dated November 2006,   and   the  Mitchell-Joyce Re-Zoning Report, dated November 2004. The area subject to this  proposed Redevelopment Plan is located in the   Valley Road and   Mitchell Street area   of  West Orange -the subject of the 2004 Report.   We have read the March 2003 Downtown  West Orange   Redevelopment   Plan and the   Plan Amendments of July 2006.   We also  reviewed the Master Plans of adjoining municipalities, including   the Township of  Livingston, Borough of Roseland, Borough of Essex Fells, Borough of Verona,  Township of   Montclair,   Township of Maplewood,   Township of Millburn, the Village of  South Orange, and the City of Orange. We have also read the   City of Orange,   Central  Valley Redevelopment Plan , dated December 2005   and Amended Plan, dated   June , 200 7 .  The area subject to this Redevelopment Plan is directly adjacent to the Central Valley  Redevelopment Plan, adopted by the City of Orange.  We have   also   reviewed the Tax Map of the Township of West Orange, dated  March 1986 ,   and the   property ta x records - tax assessment property record cards and tax  bill s .   Benecke Economics has an extensive proprietary data base of demographic   data   and  municipal tax profiles. We also are a member of the Urban Land Institute (ULI) and other  planning and economic development organizations and often consult their reports.  We have also reviewed the State of New Jersey Development and Redevelopment  Plan   (SDRP)   so as to ensure that this Redevelopment Plan is consistent thereto .

10  5 -Purpose of this Redevelopment Plan:  The purpose of this   Redevelopment Plan is to present an appropriate basis for   the  redevelopment of   this largely vacant industrial area . The   redevelop ment contemplated  shall be   consistent with the goals and objectives stated herein.   The redevelopment of this  area represents an opportunity to work with   the property owners to achieve a workable  solution to the environmental problems   apparent in the area . This Plan also represents an  opportunity to eliminate   the blighting influences   of th e   area on   adjoining residential  neighborhoods.  The Township of West Orange has a unique opportunity to work with the City of  Orange and the State of New Jersey to create a transit hub, in a village like setting, in this  general area of   W est Orange.  6 -Goals of this Redevelopment Plan :  The   goal s   of this Redevelopment Plan   include:  A. To implement the   stated desire of the Township of West Orange Planning Board  which states   “ Additional areas in the Valley Road Corridor should be considered  for redevelopment opportunities … . ”  B.   T o   eliminat e   the blighting influences of this area on the Township of   West  Orange .   This includes the   elimination of environmental contamination at the  Harvard Press site.  C. To   facilitat e   t he clean up of the east branch of Rahway River which runs  through the site.  D.   T o take advantage of the high quality mass transportation system located nearby,  especially the Highland Rail Station.  E.   Consistent with the aforementioned goals, to develop an integrated Plan, taking  into account the City of Orange, Central   V alley Redevelopment Plan and the  West Orange Downtown Redevelopment Plan.  The attainment of these goals will result in the improvement of the quality of life  and betterment of the public health, safety and welfare   of the citizens of West Orange .  7- Relationship to Definite Local Objectives  Pursuant to N.J.S.A. 40A:12A-7.a . ( 1), th is Redevelopment Plan   has the  following   definite local objectives:  A.   To provide for site clearance , remediation of environmental contamination   and  necessary   property   improvements which will improve the   appearance   and quality  of land   of the   proposed r edevelopment   area.  B.   To eliminate an underutilized area of Downtown West Orange so as to make it a  more   vibrant area.

11  C.   To encourage residences in the Township of West Orange so as to take advantage  of the Highland Rail Station , an important public transportation point .  D.   To provide for efficient infrastructure improvements - such as public utilities,  sidewalks, and the culvert of the east branch of the Rahway River   so as to  promote future   development ( s ) .  E.   To create an economic   development opportunity   by having a significant  residential   development; this will support the remainder of the downtown,  especially the Valley Road retail businesses and the Main Street Redevelopment  at the Edison Battery site.  F.   To implement the principles stated in the 2004 Master Plan Re-Examination,  which   includes:  “ 1.   E ncourage appropriate residential development, whether single family or  cluster   housing, in locations and densities that are consistent with existing  zoning and development patterns and that can be properly served by  public   roadways, utilities, and   services … .There is strong tension  between encouraging   single family housing development throughout  the Township and preserving open   space within the Township. Through  revised zoning standards, maintaining the   residential character of  the Township and preserving open space can be   compati  ble. ”  G.   To create a Plan that is compatible with the City of Orange , Central Valley  Redevelopment Plan inasmuch as the Harvard Press building located in West  Orange and a part of this Redevelopment Plan area is also located in the City of  Orange Redevelopment Plan area, adopted in December, 2005 and amended in  June, 2007.  H.   To create a sense of space and pedestrian activity; which may be the catalyst for  future improvements to properties in the vicinity of the potential redevelopment  area.  I.   To work with the private sector to achieve a high quality redevelopment project,  thereby reducing the dependency on the public sector.  8. The   Relationship to the   SDRP and the   City of Orange, Central Valley Redevelopment  Plan :  A.   N.J.S.A.40A:12A-7 a. (5) requires that a Redevelopment Plan indicate any  significant relationship to the master plans of contiguous municipalities, the county  master plan, and the SDRP. As previously mentioned we have reviewed the master plans  of the adjoining municipalities and find that only the plan of the City of Orange is  affected by the Township of West Orange Valley Road Area Redevelopment Plan. The  following sub-section of this   Revelopment   Plan describes the important interrelationship  between this   Redevelopment   Plan and the City of Orange, Central Valley   R edevelopment  Plan.

12  The Essex County Master Plan is outdated and of   little significance to this  Redevelopment Plan .  The   aforementioned   State   of   New   Jersey   Development   and   Redevelopment   Plan  (SDRP),   adopted   on   March   1,   2001,   provides   a   comprehensive   planning   framework   for  the   future   of   New   Jersey,   including   the   application   of   smart   growth   principles   throughout  the   state.   The   SDRP   calls   for   focusing   growth   in   areas   with   existing   infrastructure   and  investment,   (cities,   suburbs,   towns)   and   away   from   environmentally   sensitive   areas.   The  Township of West Orange is located in such an area.  The   SDRP   designates   the   Township   of   West   Orange   as   planning   area   one,   PA-1,  the   preferred   designation   for   redevelopment.   In   particular,   the   SDRP   recognizes   that  center   based   development   can   provide   a   location   to   receive   visitors   and   handle   the  impacts   resulting   from   economic   development   strategies .   This   is   especially   applicable   to  the proposed Redevelopment Area because of its proximity to the Highland Rail Station.  As noted in the 2001 SDRP:  “New   development   should   be   guided   into   Centers   to   preserve   open   space,  farmland   and   natural   resources   and   to   preserve   or   improve   community   character,  increase   opportunities   for   reasonably   priced   housing   and   strengthen   beneficial  economic   development   opportunities.   Directing   development   from   the   Environs   to  Centers   will   ensure   that   the   Environs   remain   in   recreational,   cultural   or   resource  -extraction   uses   or   left   undisturbed.   The   appropriate   provision   and   scaling   of  public   facilities   and   services   should   maintain   the   integrity   and   function   of   the  ecological   systems   in   this   area.   Strategic   planning   and   investing   also   can  accommodate   beneficial   development   and   redevelopment   in   Centers,   both  efficiently and equitably. ”   (SDRP, p. 217)  The   redevelopment   of   the   proposed   area   in   need   of   redevelopment,   located   at   the  southern   section   of   Downtown   West   Orange,   is   consistent   with   the   goals   and   objectives  of   the   SDRP   adopted   pursuant   to   Law,   the   New   Jersey   Planning   Act   and   the  aforementioned   Rules   and   Regulations.   As   recommended   in   the   SDRP,   redevelopment  can   be   an   effective   implementation   strategy   to   strengthen   existing   centers,   and   other  developed areas , such as West Orange . The SDRP policies include:  “Encourage   appropriate   redevelopment   in   existing   centers   and   existing  developed   areas   that   have   the   potential   to   become   centers,   or   in   ways   to   support  center-based   development   to   accommodate   growth   that   would   otherwise   occur   in  Environs.   Redevelop   with   intensities   sufficient   to   support   transit,   a   broad   range   of  uses,   efficient   use   of   infrastructure,   and   design   that   enhance   public   safety,

13  encourage   pedestrian   activity,   reduce   dependency   on   the   automobile   and  maintain the rural character of the centers. ”   (SDRP, p. 210)  Redevelopment   is   also   recognized   as   a   legitimate   planning   tool   and   a   stated   goal  of the SDRP is to:  “Encourage   environmentally   appropriate   redevelopment   in   existing   Centers   and  existing   developed   areas   that   have   the   potential   to   become   Centers   or   in   ways  that   support   Center-based   development   to   accommodate   growth   that   would  otherwise   occur   in   the   Environs.   Redevelop   with   intensities   sufficient   to   support  transit,   a   range   of   uses   broad   enough   to   encourage   activity   beyond   the   traditional  workday,   efficient   use   of   infrastructure,   and   physical   design   features   that   enhance  public   safety,   encourage   pedestrian   activity   and   reduce   dependency   on   the  automobile   to   attract   growth   otherwise   planned   for   the   Environs.”   (SDRP,   p.  219)  By   redeveloping   the   Valley   Road,   Harvard   Press   area   and   cleaning   up   an  environmental ly   contaminat ed   area ,   this   Redevelopment   Plan   is   consistent   with   the   land  use   planning   goals   stated   in   the   SDRP   as   well   as   the   Township’s   2004   Master   Plan  Reexamination .   It   is   clear   that   by   adopting   this   Revelopment   Plan,   t he   Township   of   West  Orange   can   achieve   the   type   of   re development   favored   by   the   State   Planning   Commission  and promulgated in the SDRP, pursuant to the State Planning Act .  B . The   City of   Orange ,   Central Valley Redevelopment   Plan:   The City of Orange,  Central   Valley   Redevelopment Plan area encompasses approximately forty two (42)  acres, including that portion of the Harvard Press Building located in the City of Orange  (again, this building is also located in the Township of West Orange).   Because property  located in the Central Valley Redevelopment area   needed to be   acquired ,   the City of  Orange   issued a request for proposals and subsequently designated Harvard   Development  Associates, LLC as the redeveloper of the portion of the Central Valley Redevelopment  Area that borders on the Township of West Orange.   (This includes the portion of the  Harvard Press structure which is located in the City of Orange).   Harvard Development  Associates then prepared a concept site plan for the area, resulting in certain of the City  of Orange, Central Valley Redevelopment Plan Amendments, dated June, 2007.  Of particular importance   to the Township of West Orange, and this  Redevelopment Plan,   is the   South Jefferson Street portion of the City of Orange   P lan.  The June, 2007 City of Orange Plan amendments provide:

14  “ the conversion and adaptive reuse of the Harvard Printing Company structure,  into artist live/work spaces, new condominium construction, provision of 210 parking spaces,  and, the realignment and reopening of the Rahway River … . ”  This portion of the   City of   Orange, Central Valley Redevelopment Plan includes  the portion of the Harvard Press structure located in Orange. The aforementioned City of  Orange Plan indicates that   221   residences are to be built on that site.   According to the  parcel list ,   included in the   City of   Orange ,   Central Valley Redevelopment Plan ,   the land  area of the Harvard Press Property located in   the City of   Orange is   approximately   95,800  square feet, or slightly greater than two acres. The construction of   221   residential units on  the Harvard Press property located Orange represents a density of 100 units per acre.  A coordinated redevelopment effort between West Orange and Orange, with the  participation of the State of New Jersey, will enhance the public transportation service at  the Highland Rail Station, while at the same time making this general area more vibrant  and productive.  9.   The   Private Sector ’ s Role in the Harvard Press Site :  The development community has expressed interest in the Harvard Press site,  prompted in part by the redevelopment efforts being undertaken in the adjoining City of  Orange. For example,   Harvard Development Associates, LLC,   which is the owner and  redeveloper of the adjoining Orange site, has executed contracts to purchase the  properties in the Area,   and has received a Stream Encroachment and Freshwater  Wetlands permit from the New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection to  improve the east branch of the Rahway River, including the waterway culvert, within the  Township. A portion of the culvert lies under the Harvard Press structure.  The pursuit of simultaneous redevelopment efforts of these adjoining parcels by  their respective municipalities is a sound planning principle that enhances the likelihood  of a comprehensive, cohesive, rational, successful development effort for each  municipality. The interest in redevelopment of the surrounding area expressed by the City  of Orange and the interest expressed on both sides of the municipal border by the  development community present   an opportunity for the Township of West Orange to  work with the private sector to clean up the   A rea and remove the persistent bligh ting  influences present in the A rea. By doing so the Township will achieve an objective stated  in this Plan, specifically, Objective I.

15  10 .   Proposed Land Uses and Building Requirements in the Redevelopment Area.  A. Land Use :   The   current   zoning of the proposed   redevelopment   area is Industrial ( “ I ” ).  The proposed redevelopment area is located within Block 9 of the Township of West  Orange as indicated on the official Tax Map of the Township of West Orange. The  proposed redevelopment area encompasses approximately one third of Block 9. It is  significant that this Block is divided between zoning districts: the aforementioned I zone  and the B-1 zone . The B-1 zone   includes those properties having frontage along Central  Avenue , to the west of the proposed redevelopment area . The B-1   zone   is a retail  business zoning district.  We note that Mitchell-Joyce Rezoning Report, dated November 2004, indicates  the specific need to redevelop the Harvard Press industrial building. Once again, this  building   is a four and one half story ostensibly abandoned structure. The Mitchell-Joyce  Rezoning Report notes that the nearby street of Mitchell, which borders this  Redevelopment Plan area, is residential in nature. This Report also suggests a residential  building plan of four stories over one story of parking   is appropriate   (see Exhibit 5,  therein).   This is a similar building configuration as   is incorporated   in to   the City of  Orange, Central Valley Redevelopment Plan.  We note that th e West Orange, Valley Road Redevelopment Area   is in a 100 year  flood hazard zone and may require the ground floor of any new structure   to   be elevated.  Therefore, having parking located on the ground floor, provided there is adequate  ventilation ,   makes sense.   The suggestions of the Mitchell-Joyce Report have merit and, in  part, support our research leading to our recommended land use plan, to be included in  the Valley Road Area Redevelopment Plan.  Th e current   zoning configuration ,   when   considered along   with the redevelopment  of the propose d   area ,   presents an opportunity for the Township of West Orange to create  a mixed-use redevelopment zone, by combining the retail uses   permitted in the B-1 zone  with a residential use at the proposed redevelopment area.   By creating a mixed use zone  the Township of West Orange will eliminate the underutilized and blighted industrial  zone and replace it with a new, vibrant   M ixed   U se Business   Residential zone.   This type

16  of land use pattern is envisioned in both the aforementioned Mitchell-Joyce Report and  the Master Plan Reexamination of 2004.  With the implementation of this land use plan t wo zoning   “ anchors ”   will be in  place; the pre - existing B-1 zone which includes seven (7) properties in Block 9 and the  proposed Redevelopment Plan zone which include the five (5) properties included in this  Plan ,   and also located in Block 9 . These   two   property   categories   total twelve (12)  properties. Thirteen (13) properties   located in Block 9   will remain   in the I zone , they are  located in the middle of the two anchors .   The se thirteen   properties should be examined to  determine if they should be   re-zoned and included in   the Mixed Use Business Residential  zone. Our initial impression is that they should be, and, with the cooperation of the  property owners, a development or redevelopment scenario   may possibly   be created to  further achieve the objectives of this Plan, specifically   O bjective H.  Pursuant to N.J.S.A. 40A:12A-7.a.(2), the proposed land uses and building  requirements in the   Township of West Orange   Valley Road   Redevelopment Area are   to  be governed by   a new   the   Mixed Use Business   Residential   zone. Because of the   limited  size of the proposed redevelopment area, and due to the land use and building  configurations included in the City of Orange, Central Valley Redevelopment Plan,   it is  recommended that the following parcels be zoned Mixed Use Business Residential  (MUBR):   Block 9, Lots 1, 7, 44, 50, and 56.  This re zoning should be made part of the Redevelopment Plan Ordinance. The  Redevelopment Plan Ordinance should make specific reference to the following  properties located in the retail business zone, B-1 , they are: Block 9, Lots 18, 21, 23, 25,  26, 27, and 28. These properties and perhaps other properties located in the B-1 zone  along Valley Road provide an important   linkage   to the residences being proposed in the  Valley Road   Area   Redevelopment Plan.   The   businesses   will provide services to the  residents and in turn the residents will support the businesses. This will   create an  economic engine in this area of the West Orange Downtown and   help achieve the  objectives of this Plan, specifically   O bjective E.  The Zoning Map of the Township of West Orange should be amended (modified)  upon adoption by the Township Council of the Valley Road Area Redevelopment Plan to  depict this new zone.

17  At this time th e Township of West Orange may not desire to re - zone the  aforementioned properties located in Block 9   that are   part of the B-1 zone .   H owever,   at a  minimum ,   further research should be   performed   as to   the efficacy of   including these  properties in the MUBR zone.   The same holds true for the aforementioned thirteen (13)  properties located in Block 9 between the Redevelopment Plan Area and the B-1 zone.  This new, proposed zoning district ,   MUBR ) is   consistent with the New Jersey  Municipal land Use Law (MLUL), N.J.S.A. 40:55D et seq.   inasmuch as the zone is  designed to promote a consistent land use   pattern.  Permitted uses in the MUBR zone shall be limited to multi - family residential uses  and   those   uses allowed in the B-1 zone.   The residential units shall be condominium type  units that are individually sold in the general real estate market place and not rented.   This  use provision may need to be amended upon completi ng the research   of the   remaining  properties   located   in Block 9 and along Central Avenue. Any use not expressly permitted  in the MUBR zone shall be prohibited , including rental units .
